ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a nasi lemak entry (FOODON_00002809) but does not have a specific variant for nasi lemak with mutton; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers nasi lemak as a dish category; the mutton variant is not separately coded. Closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for nasi lemak or its mutton variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi lemak with mutton curry, comprising approximately 200 g coconut rice, 80 g mutton curry, and accompaniments (sambal, fried anchovies, peanuts, egg, cucumber) totalling approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak (with meat) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists nasi lemak with chicken and nasi lemak with fish. The mutton variant is not separately listed. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB nasi lemak with meat (chicken) as the closest structural analogue for the rice-and-accompaniments base, with protein and fat values adjusted upward to reflect mutton's higher fat and protein density per ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (mutton, cooked). Per-100g values represent the composite dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice-based mixed dishes — coconut rice (nasi lemak) analogous estimate based on white rice cooked with coconut milk
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi lemak with mutton. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for white rice in the range 64–72 and for coconut milk rice preparations in the range 50–58. Nasi lemak rice is cooked with coconut milk and pandan, which lowers the GI relative to plain white rice due to fat content slowing gastric emptying. A GI of 55 is used as a conservative estimate for the composite dish, consistent with published analogous coconut rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (55 × 82 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 45.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal, fried anchovies, and spice paste
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving of nasi lemak with mutton provides approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced sambal and anchovies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from coconut rice combined with saturated fat from coconut milk and mutton
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 55 and GL of approximately 45 per serving indicate a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a plate, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut milk and mutton, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 18.4 g saturated fat per 400 g serving, exceeding the American Heart Association's recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2000 kcal diet; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in reduced portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak with Mutton is a Singaporean and Malaysian dish consisting of fragrant coconut milk rice served with braised or curried mutton and traditional accompaniments. The rice is cooked with coconut milk, pandan leaves, and sometimes lemongrass, giving it a rich, creamy flavour and slightly sticky texture. The mutton component is typically slow-cooked in a spiced curry or rendang-style gravy containing cumin, coriander, chilli, turmeric, and ginger, resulting in tender, deeply flavoured meat. Standard accompaniments include sambal chilli paste, crispy fried anchovies (ikan bilis), roasted peanuts, a hard-boiled or fried egg, and fresh cucumber slices. This variant is particularly popular in Indian-Muslim (Mamak) and Malay-Indian fusion eateries in Singapore, such as Baba Eating House. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with notable saturated fat from coconut milk and mutton, moderate protein from the meat and egg, and a moderate glycemic load. The high sodium content from sambal and anchovies is a clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-certified in most Singaporean establishments serving it.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists specifically for nasi lemak with mutton. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB nasi lemak with meat (chicken) as the closest structural analogue for the rice base and accompaniments, with protein and fat adjusted using ICMR-NIN cooked mutton values. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney coconut rice data. Confidence is medium due to the composite adaptation rather than a direct source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or ICMR-NIN sources used. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated values from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy for mutton and rice components. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Meat and alternatives' (input) to 'Mixed' as nasi lemak is a composite rice-based dish. Cuisine has been corrected from 'Indian' (input) to 'Singaporean' as nasi lemak is a Singaporean/Malaysian national dish; the Indian-Muslim (Mamak) preparation style is noted in the description. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the Veritas threshold rule (≤55 = low). GL per serving = (55 × 82.0 g available carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 45.1.
